EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon convection. Falling freezing levels contributed to the formation of large hail with many of these storms. All severe reports were exclusively due to hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Windham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon convection. Falling freezing levels contributed to the formation of large hail with many of these storms. All severe reports were exclusively due to hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching cold front to produce over 6 inches of rain in 2 to 4 hours causing tremendous flash flooding in eastern Cumberland County. Roads were washed out or flooded by several feet of water. Cars were submerged and evacuations ordered. In Portland the flash flooding forced the evacuation of eight properties in the city including 200 people who were staying at the La Quinta Inn on Park Avenue. In Portland over 5 inches of rain fell in 4 hours with 1.42 inches falling in 27 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding washing out numerous roads in the Freeport area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. Many roads were washed out or flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Topsham resulting in flooded roads and washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. A few roads were washed out or flooded in several towns in Kennebec County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in Litchfield washing out or flooding a few roads in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A January thaw that lasted from the 13th to the 16th of the month sent temperatures to near 50 degrees over the 3 day period. Temperatures remained above freezing for almost 48 hours. In addition, 1 to 2 inches of rain fell from the 11th to the 12th with an additional 0.75 to 1.5 inches from the 14th to the 15th. This caused ice to move and jam on the Kennebec River at North Sidney resulting in a brief period of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt and heavy rain caused ice to jam on the Kennebec River at North Sidney (flood stage 17.0 ft), which crested at 17.23 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air aloft with an upper trof and diurnal heating combined to produce enough instability to produce showers and thunderstorms across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 7th. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in southern Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.00 inch in diameter occurred near the intersection of Kingdom Road and Southern Bay Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were toppled on Pushaw Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in flash flooding in Central Maine. Flooding occurred in both Lewiston and Winthrop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Lewiston stalling cars in flood waters on Maple Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced 2 to 3 inches of rain resulting in flash flooding in Central Maine. Flooding occurred in both Lewiston and Winthrop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Winthrop. Route 133 was reduced to one lane with water flowing over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a large tree blocking traffic on Route 129 in Damariscotta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking well west of Maine during the 11th drew warmer air north across the region. However...the low level cold air was slow to erode resulting in freezing rain. The freezing rain did gradually change to rain...from south to north...through the morning and afternoon hours of the 11th. Ice accumulations along the Downeast coast generally were .10 of an inch or less...with .10 to .25 of an inch across interior Downeast and central portions of the region. Ice accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from .20 to .30 of an inch...with local totals up to .40 of an inch. |Although ice accumulations remained below warning levels...the ice produced very hazardous driving conditions through the morning into the afternoon hours. Even after temperatures warmed above freezing...the recent cold temperatures kept ground surfaces below freezing allowing ice to continue to accumulate. Hundreds of accidents occurred across the region. Portions of I-95 were closed for a time along with other roads. A passenger jet slid off the taxiway at Bangor International Airport while turning to approach a gate when the nose wheel lost traction. No injuries occurred on the plane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced very hazardous driving conditions which contributed to accidents.

Snow developed during the afternoon of the 25th and continued into the early morning hours of the 26th. Warnings verified during the late evening hours of the 25th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure which had begun to build across the region during late December persisted into early January. Temperatures from the 1st into the morning of the 4th averaged 25 to 40 degrees below normal. The coldest overnight low temperatures generally occurred during the morning of the 2nd with readings ranging from 25 below to around 40 below zero across northern areas with 10 below to 20 below zero Downeast. A record daily low temperature of 28 below zero was recorded at Caribou during the morning of the 2nd...with a record daily low of 19 below zero established at Bangor. Otherwise...low temperatures during this time generally ranged from 20 below to 35 below zero across northern areas...with 5 below to 15 below zero Downeast. Temperatures remained below zero for 48 to 72 hours across portions of northern Maine during this time. The coldest day also occurred on the 2nd when high temperatures across northern areas generally held in the 10 below to 20 below zero range...with single digits below zero Downeast. Winds occasionally combined with the extreme cold to produce wind chills of 45 below to 55 below zero across northern areas and 30 below to 40 below zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought extremely cold temperatures to the region at the start of January. Dangerous wind chills also occurred at times. This was the continuation of an extreme cold event which began at the end of December.

Ice accumulations along the Downeast coast generally were .10 of an inch or less...with .10 to .25 of an inch across interior Downeast and central portions of the region. Ice accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from .20 to .30 of an inch...with local totals up to .40 of an inch. |Although ice accumulations remained below warning levels...the ice produced very hazardous driving conditions through the morning into the afternoon hours. Even after temperatures warmed above freezing...the recent cold temperatures kept ground surfaces below freezing allowing ice to continue to accumulate. Hundreds of accidents occurred across the region. Portions of I-95 were closed for a time along with other roads. A passenger jet slid off the taxiway at Bangor International Airport while turning to approach a gate when the nose wheel lost traction. No injuries occurred on the plane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced very hazardous driving conditions which contributed to accidents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill readings ranged from 35 below to 45 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked northwest of Maine from late on the 5th through the 6th. Warmer air was drawn north. However...low level cold air was slow to erode resulting in freezing rain across Downeast areas. Light freezing rain developed during the evening of the 5th and continued through the early morning hours of the 6th. Ice accumulations along the Downeast coast were generally .10 of an inch or less...with .10 to .25 of an inch across interior Downeast areas. Temperatures then rose above freezing during the morning of the 6th with precipitation transitioning to rain.|A strong low level jet also crossed the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 6th. Occasional wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported along the Downeast coast...with generally 35 to 45 mph gusts across interior Downeast areas. The combination of ice on trees and the gusty winds caused branches to snap and also toppled some trees. The warming temperatures also caused ice to unload from trees on to power lines. The shedding ice was partially from this event along with lingering ice from the major ice storm of late December. Snapped branches...falling trees and shedding ice contributed to power outages. At the peak of the event...around 4000 customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds and ice combined to snap branches and bring down trees on power lines which contributed to power outages.

Ice accumulations along the Downeast coast were generally .10 of an inch or less...with .10 to .25 of an inch across interior Downeast areas. Temperatures then rose above freezing during the morning of the 6th with precipitation transitioning to rain.|A strong low level jet also crossed the region during the morning and early afternoon hours of the 6th. Occasional wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported along the Downeast coast...with generally 35 to 45 mph gusts across interior Downeast areas. The combination of ice on trees and the gusty winds caused branches to snap and also toppled some trees. The warming temperatures also caused ice to unload from trees on to power lines. The shedding ice was partially from this event along with lingering ice from the major ice storm of late December. Snapped branches...falling trees and shedding ice contributed to power outages. At the peak of the event...around 4000 customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds and ice combined to snap branches and bring down trees on power lines which contributed to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracking well west of Maine during the 11th drew warmer air north across the region. However...the low level cold air was slow to erode resulting in freezing rain. The freezing rain did gradually change to rain...from south to north...through the morning and afternoon hours of the 11th. Ice accumulations along the Downeast coast generally were .10 of an inch or less...with .10 to .25 of an inch across interior Downeast and central portions of the region. Ice accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from .20 to .30 of an inch...with local totals up to .40 of an inch. |Although ice accumulations remained below warning levels...the ice produced very hazardous driving conditions through the morning into the afternoon hours. Even after temperatures warmed above freezing...the recent cold temperatures kept ground surfaces below freezing allowing ice to continue to accumulate. Hundreds of accidents occurred across the region. Portions of I-95 were closed for a time along with other roads. A passenger jet slid off the taxiway at Bangor International Airport while turning to approach a gate when the nose wheel lost traction. No injuries occurred on the plane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced very hazardous driving conditions which contributed to accidents.

Low pressure developed along the mid Atlantic region during the 26th then rapidly intensified while tracking toward southern Nova Scotia from the overnight hours of the 26th into the 27th. The low then exited across the maritimes during the morning of the 27th. A wintry mix rapidly transitioned to snow across Downeast areas during the early afternoon of the 26th. Snow then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the rest of the afternoon and evening of the 26th. Snow persisted through the overnight hours of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met across much of the region during the late evening hours of the 26th and across northern areas during the early morning hours of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches across interior Downeast and central portions of the region...with 6 to 11 inches from central Piscataquis...across northern Penobscot to eastern Aroostook counties. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph...with a few higher gusts along the coast...occurred across Downeast areas. The winds and heavy wet snow contributed to around 9000 customers losing power across Hancock and southern portions of Penobscot counties from the evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 10 inches. Wind gusts generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph...with a few higher gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked from the mid Atlantic coast to Nova Scotia from the night of the 1st through the 2nd...then lifted across eastern New Brunswick toward Labrador through the night of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. A rain and snow mix transitioned to heavy wet snow across Downeast areas during the early morning hours of the 2nd...with snow then expanding northward across the remainder of the region through the rest of the morning. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 2nd. The snow persisted into the early morning hours of the 3rd. The heaviest storm total snow accumulations of 10 to 18 inches occurred in areas from southern and central Penobscot...to northern Washington and southeast Aroostook counties. Total accumulations of 7 to 13 inches occurred from northern Penobscot to northeast Aroostook counties and across Hancock county. Warmer air drawn inland limited accumulations across coastal and central portions of Washington county. Record daily snowfalls occurred at both Caribou and Bangor during the 2nd...with 10.1 inches at Caribou and 12.0 inches at Bangor.|Heavy wet snow clinging to trees and power lines along with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph contributed to major power outages Downeast with snapped branches and toppled trees. Power outages began to develop during the morning of the 2nd then steadily increased through the remainder of the 2nd into the 3rd. At their peak...during the morning of the 3rd...around 60000 customers were without power mostly across Penobscot and Hancock counties. The Governor declared a state of emergency in order to help with power restoration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 11 to 17 inches. Wind gusts ranged from 35 to 45 mph.

Low pressure developed along the mid Atlantic region during the 26th then rapidly intensified while tracking toward southern Nova Scotia from the overnight hours of the 26th into the 27th. The low then exited across the maritimes during the morning of the 27th. A wintry mix rapidly transitioned to snow across Downeast areas during the early afternoon of the 26th. Snow then expanded northward across the remainder of the region through the rest of the afternoon and evening of the 26th. Snow persisted through the overnight hours of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met across much of the region during the late evening hours of the 26th and across northern areas during the early morning hours of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 13 inches across interior Downeast and central portions of the region...with 6 to 11 inches from central Piscataquis...across northern Penobscot to eastern Aroostook counties. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph...with a few higher gusts along the coast...occurred across Downeast areas. The winds and heavy wet snow contributed to around 9000 customers losing power across Hancock and southern portions of Penobscot counties from the evening of the 26th into the morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 14 inches. Wind gusts ranged from 30 to 40 mph.

An area of heavy precipitation developed across Washington county within the deformation zone of an upper low during the afternoon of the 31st. Precipitation early in the afternoon was a mix of light rain and freezing rain. Temperatures aloft cooled while the precipitation intensified allowing an initial transition to heavy sleet and then heavy snow through the mid to late afternoon hours. Sleet rapidly accumulated 1 to 2 inches in a short time. Warning criteria sleet accumulations were met during the late afternoon. The sleet transitioned to an area of very heavy snow during the late afternoon and evening which eventually focused into a heavier band with lighter snows on either side. Winds occasionally gusted to 30 to 40 mph during the time of heaviest snow. The heavy snow and blowing snow reduced visibilities below a quarter mile at times during the late afternoon into the evening creating hazardous driving conditions. Snow intensities diminished during the evening along with the winds. However...lighter snows persisted into the early morning hours of April 1st. Storm total snow accumulations through the early morning hours of April 1st ranged from 4 to 8 inches within the heavier band...with 3 to 5 inch accumulations on either side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. The event continued into the early morning hours of April 1st.

A cold front crossed the region from the late morning through the afternoon hours of the 26th along with an upper level disturbance and cooling temperature aloft. Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front during the late morning and persisted through late afternoon. The severe thunderstorms produced large hail. A weak tornado  was also reported near Ludlow in southeast Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered the road leading to Timmony Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front spread rain across the region during the evening hours of May 16th. The rain continued through the night and ended by noon on the 17th. In general 2 to 3 inches of rain fell with a maximum of 3.41 inches reported in the mountains of Maine. This caused minor flooding on several small rivers and streams in Oxford County including the Ellis River at South Andover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dumped 2 to 3 inches of rain in the mountains of Maine resulting in flooding of small rivers and streams across Oxford County including the Ellis River at South Andover (flood stage 16.0 ft), which crested at 16.42 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Developing low pressure crossed the Gulf of Maine from the overnight hours of the 18th into the 19th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of temperatures in the 50s and 60s caused rapid snow melt and resulted in an ice jam and minor flooding on the Carrabassett River at North Anson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures resulted in rapid snow melt and a ice jam on the Carrabassett River at North Anson (flood stage 15.0 ft), producing minor flooding before cresting at 15.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures near 80 along with 1 to 2 inches of rain caused the remaining snowpack to quickly melt resulting in widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Carrabassett River at North Anson (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 15.83 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Limington during the afternoon of July 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Limington during the afternoon of July 28th.  The tornado touched down near Paul's Way near Limington, then moved northeast for more than 1.5 miles through mainly conservation land.  Numerous large trees, some 16 to 24 inches in diameter, were either snapped or uprooted along the intermittent path of damage.  The tornado's damage path was up to 80 yards wide and maximum wind gusts were estimated at about 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Industry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a 16-year-old boy in Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 16-year-old boy was injured when he was struck by lightning at the Four Seasons Campground in Naples, Maine.  The boy was sitting outside at a picnic table when lightning struck a nearby tree and apparently travel along the ground to the boy. The boy was transported to the hospital and later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm sparked a fire in Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire that destroyed a home on Chipmunk Lane in Naples.  The lightning struck two tall pine trees 30 feet from the home and found its way to the attic area of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire in an apartment building in Farmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck one of the apartment buildings at the Blueberry Hills Apartments in Farmington and ignited a fire that caused an estimated 100,000 in damages.  About 13 tenants of the four damaged apartments were displaced by the fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1.25 inch hail in the Stroudwater section of Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief well defined funnel cloud occurred near the town of Wallagrass in northern Aroostook county during the early evening of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed along a warm front lifting north across the region. A slow moving thunderstorm with rainfall rates of around 2.00 inches per hour produced flash flooding in the town of Dexter in southern Penobscot county during the evening of the 15th. The flash flooding washed out several dirt roads and flooded basements.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm with rainfall rates of around 2.00 inches per hour produced flash flooding in Dexter. The flash flooding washed out several dirt roads and flooded basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout touched down briefly over Sebago Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout touched down over Sebago Lake during the early evening of July 27.  Residents got several pictures of the waterspout.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The post tropical remnants of what had once been Hurricane Arthur brought high winds and heavy rain to eastern Maine. The strongest winds occurred through the morning hours of the 5th across Washington and Hancock counties. Peak wind gusts across Washington and Hancock counties generally ranged from around 40 mph up to around 60 mph...with the highest gusts generally across Washington county nearest the track of the low. A peak wind gust of 71 mph was reported at Machias Seal Island well offshore from Washington county. Extensive tree damage occurred across Washington and Hancock counties with trees uprooted or snapped along with numerous broken branches. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines and utility poles which led to extensive power outages. At the peak of the event...20000 to 25000 customers lost power across mostly Washington and Hancock counties with lesser power outages across Penobscot county. Some customers across Washington county were without power for several days.|Another area of damaging winds developed across portions of eastern Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 5th where heavy rain helped mix strong winds aloft to the surface. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph occurred across portions of eastern Aroostook county where extensive tree damage also occurred with many trees uprooted or snapped along with many broken branches and scattered power outages. Fully leafed trees and wet ground enhanced the impact of the tree damage from the winds across all of eastern Maine.  |Heavy rain also occurred across portions of Eastern Maine. Abundant moisture was drawn north along a stalled frontal boundary in advance of the low. Heavy rain developed across portions of Washington and Hancock counties during the evening of the 4th and persisted into the afternoon of the 5th. Event total rainfall across Washington county generally ranged from 3.00 to 5.00 inches...with localized totals of 6.00 to 6.50 inches. Rainfall totals across Hancock county generally ranged from 3.00 to 4.00 inches. Minor small stream flooding along with ponding of water in poor drainage areas occurred across Washington and Hancock counties. Heavy rains also developed across eastern Aroostook county from the overnight hours of the 4th into the morning of the 5th and persisted into the afternoon. Rainfall totals across eastern Aroostook county generally ranged from 1.50 to 3.50 inches...with localized totals of up to 4.00 to 4.50 inches. Ponding of water in poor drainage areas was reported across portions of eastern Aroostook county...though no significant flooding issues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts produced extensive tree damage with many trees uprooted or snapped along with numerous broken branches. The falling trees and branches helped bring down power lines and utility poles which led to scattered power outages. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 40 mph up to around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours resulting in many flooded streets, road washouts and flooded businesses in Somerset County. The Madison Police station was flooded during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced 3 to 4 inches of rain in Madison washing out roads and flooding streets and businesses in town. The Madison Police station was flooded in this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours resulting in many flooded streets, road washouts and flooded businesses in Somerset County. The Madison Police station was flooded during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Anson resulting in flooded streets and road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours resulting in many flooded streets, road washouts and flooded businesses in Somerset County. The Madison Police station was flooded during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in Starks resulting in many flooded streets and road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The downdraft from a severe thunderstorm caused a microburst in the town of York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced microburst winds in the village of York.  The microburst affected an area of about 1 mile long and about 1.5 miles wide.  Hundreds  of trees were snapped or uprooted in the town.  Two homes suffered major damage with 12 homes having moderate to minor damage.  Additionally, 28 properties had damage to other buildings, sheds, fences and pools.  Three vehicles were destroyed by falling trees with 14 more that sustained moderate to minor damage.  The hardest hit areas were along Donica Road, Rayden Road, Fieldstone Estates Road, and Route 1-A where winds were estimated to have gusted to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass combined with an approaching cold front to produce over 6 inches of rain in 2 to 4 hours causing tremendous flash flooding in eastern Cumberland County. Roads were washed out or flooded by several feet of water. Cars were submerged and evacuations ordered. In Portland the flash flooding forced the evacuation of eight properties in the city including 200 people who were staying at the La Quinta Inn on Park Avenue. In Portland over 5 inches of rain fell in 4 hours with 1.42 inches falling in 27 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Pownal washing out several area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. Many roads were washed out or flooded and 1 person was recued from a submerged car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in less than three hours causing flash flooding in Lewiston washing out numerous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season Nor'easter developed and moved into the Gulf of Maine early on the 14th. This system produced a prolonged period of gale force winds and building seas.|In addition, very high astronomical tides from a super moon combined with the low pressure system to produce minor coastal flooding across isolated locations in southwest Maine. The tide in Portland Harbor topped out right at its 12 foot flood stage, resulting in very minor flooding of side streets along the wharfs. In Biddeford, very minor flooding occurred along side Granite Point road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early season Nor'easter combined with high astronomical tides to produce minor coastal flooding in Biddeford. Very minor flooding occurred alongside Granite Point Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 2 to 3 inches in one hour resulting in many flooded streets and washed out roads. Route 2 in Mexico flooded as well as Route 45 in Farmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooded streets and road washouts in Rumford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 2 to 3 inches in one hour resulting in many flooded streets and washed out roads. Route 2 in Mexico flooded as well as Route 45 in Farmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Mexico resulting in many flooded streets and road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced 2 to 3 inches in one hour resulting in many flooded streets and washed out roads. Route 2 in Mexico flooded as well as Route 45 in Farmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on Route 43 in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. Many roads were washed out or flooded and 1 person was recued from a submerged car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in less than three hours causing flash flooding in Wales with numerous roads flooded or washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. Many roads were washed out or flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell in less than three hours causing many road washouts and flooded streets in the Bowdoin area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front set the stage for strong afternoon convection. Wind damage was the main threat with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in Rangeley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped a tree on a house on East Bridge Street in Westbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the mid-Atlantic coast on the morning of the 2nd and then intensified as it moved northeast through the Gulf of Maine on the 3rd.  The storm brought a 6 to 14 inches of snow across the southwestern part of the state.

Low pressure passing west of New England set up a modest southerly wind flow gradually increasing seas. The highest astronomical tides of the month combined with the increasing seas to produce minor coastal flooding across isolated locations in southwest Maine. The tide in Portland Harbor topped out right at the 12.0 foot flood stage, resulting in very minor flooding of side streets along the wharfs. In Biddeford, very minor flooding occurred alongside Granite Point Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A modest southerly flow and rising seas combined with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce minor coastal flooding in Portland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A modest southerly flow and rising seas combined with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce minor flooding in the Biddeford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid air mass remained in place across the region on the afternoon of July 3rd. A slow moving cold front to the west of the area triggered afternoon convection which quickly became severe across a large portion of New Hampshire. A few isolated severe thunderstorms pushed into western Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Raymond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus of afternoon convection. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Rabbit Valley Road and on King Street in Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus of afternoon convection. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree and wires on Libby and Patterson Roads in Mechanic Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus of afternoon convection. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Empire Road in Poland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus of afternoon convection. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed three trees and a transformer on a road near Athens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong shear and moderate capes produced an isolated severe thunderstorm in York County which produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees at the Storrs Ski Area in East Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus of afternoon convection. The main threat with these storms was damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on wires in Poland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon convection. Falling freezing levels contributed to the formation of large hail with many of these storms. All severe reports were exclusively due to hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Parsonsfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced 3 to 5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours resulting in flash flooding. A few roads were washed out or flooded in several towns in Kennebec County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Chelsea washing out roads and flooding streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass along with an approaching cold front produced over 6 inches of rain in 2 to 4 hours resulting in tremendous flash flooding. Many roads were washed out or flooded resulting in damages of 100,000 dollars in Brunswick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in Brunswick washing out many roads and flooding city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 2nd with a stationary front to the west of the region. This set the stage for showers and thunderstorms which produced very heavy rain. Two to four inches of rain fell in less than 2 hours in the Rumford area washing out several roads and flooding streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produce 2 to 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours in southern Oxford County. Several road washouts and flooded streets occurred in Rumford with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the town of Saint Albans during the evening of July 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the town of Saint Albans during the evening of July 15.  Damage was observed from High Street south of Indian Pond to Melody Lane.  The most intense damage was observed along Corinna Road, Hanson Drive, and Warner Lane where winds were estimated to have gusted to 80 to 90 mph.  The tornado touched down at approximately 652 pm, then moved northeast at about 30 mph.  The tornado was on the ground for about 5 minutes and travelled about 2.75 miles.  The maximum path width was estimated to be 800 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 2nd with a stationary front to the west of the region. This set the stage for showers and thunderstorms which produced very heavy rain. Two to four inches of rain fell in less than 2 hours in the Rumford area washing out several roads and flooding streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the Jay area of Franklin County. Two to four inches of rain in less than 2 hours resulted in street flooding and road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to around 30 mph and choppy waves capsized a 12 foot aluminum boat on Wytopitlock Lake during the afternoon of the 10th. The two fishermen in the boat were thrown into the water when the boat capsized. One fisherman was able to swim to safety. However...the other fisherman never reached shore and his body was recovered around two hours later. Neither man was wearing a personal flotation device.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to around 30 mph and choppy waves capsized a 12 foot aluminum boat on Wytopitlock Lake during the afternoon of the 10th. The two fishermen in the boat were thrown into the water when the boat capsized. One fisherman was able to swim to safety. However...the other fisherman never reached shore and his body was recovered around two hours later. Neither man was wearing a personal flotation device.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of low pressure combined to produce heavy wet snow and high winds across Sagadahoc, Lincoln, Knox, Waldo, Kennebec, and southern Somerset Counties.  The first low produced mainly rain across the region Saturday night, but pulled colder air into the region from the north after it had passed.  The second low brought heavy wet snow during the day Sunday into Sunday evening. The weight of the wet snow and high winds caused numerous widespread and long-lasting power outages across the region.  The number of power outages reached a peak Monday morning (November 3) when 84,000 customers were without power.  All totaled, an estimated 166,000 Central Maine Power Company customers lost electrical service during the event. Storm conditions lingered into Sunday night, and the number of outages reached a peak of 84,000 just before 8 a.m. Monday. ||Storm damage was particularly severe in Lincoln, Knox, and Waldo Counties in Maine���s mid-coast region, where up to a foot-and-a-half of snow fell in some areas. Trees and limbs came down onto power lines, breaking more than 170 utility poles and bringing down hundreds of sections of wire. Snow-coated roads made travel conditions hazardous and combined with storm debris to keep crews from accessing many of the damaged areas. Persistent moderate winds caused new outages on Monday as crews worked to assess damage and restore service to major distribution lines. By midnight, Thursday night, service was back to more than 95 percent of customers affected by Sunday���s storm. The remaining outages cleaned up on Thursday.  The CMP restoration team included more than 260 two-person line repair crews and 110 tree crews, backed up by hundreds of support personnel in the field and company offices.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Main Street in Brownfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Explosively intensifying low pressure tracked east of the Gulf of Maine and Nova Scotia during the 26th into the early morning hours of the 27th. The storm produced blizzard conditions across portions of Downeast Maine during the 26th...with the worst conditions occurring from the late morning through the evening hours. Blizzard warning criteria was met during the afternoon of the 26th...with blizzard conditions persisting through the evening of the 26th. Lighter snow...along with areas of blowing and drifting snow...then continued into the early morning hours of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations across central and coastal Washington county ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with 1 to 3 inches across northern Washington county and coastal Hancock county. A sharp edge to the snow gradient existed to the northwest of these areas with snow totals rapidly tapering to less than an inch. |The most significant impacts from the blizzard occurred across Washington county along with coastal Hancock county. Several roads were closed in these areas due to significant blowing and drifting snow along with high snow drifts and hazardous driving conditions. The strongest winds occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th with sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph and wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph. The strong winds toppled trees and power lines in scattered areas. In Bar Harbor a tree was toppled onto a police car during the late afternoon. The officer was not injured. In Jonesport a boat was torn from its mooring and sunk in the harbor during the evening. |Wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph occurred across the remainder of Downeast Maine. However...with snow accumulations of generally an inch or less impacts were less extensive. Visibilities in these areas were reduced at times along with lesser blowing and drifting snow. The strong winds did topple trees and power lines in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds produced significant blowing and drifting snow along with blizzard conditions. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine during the night of the 12th...exiting across the maritimes during the 13th. Snow expanded north across the region through the afternoon of the 12th. Snow...heavy at times...then continued into the afternoon of the 13th. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met through the evening hours of the 12th into the early morning hours of the 13th. Storm total snow accumulations across the region generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with localized totals of around 20 inches. Heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines contributed to power outages across Downeast areas...where around 7000 customers lost power. The most extensive power outages occurred around Bangor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 14 to 19 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Mid-West into southeastern Canada from the 19th through the 21st as a secondary low developed off the New England Coast and moved northeast.  The secondary low brought heavy snow to the mountains and foothills of Western Maine with 6 to 18 inches of snow across the foothills and the southern slopes of the mountains from the evening of the 19th through the morning of the 20th.

An area of heavy precipitation developed across central and coastal portions of Washington county within the deformation zone of an upper low during the afternoon of March 31st. Precipitation early in the afternoon was a mix of light rain and freezing rain. Temperatures aloft cooled while the precipitation intensified allowing an initial transition to heavy sleet and then heavy snow through the mid to late afternoon hours. Sleet rapidly accumulated 1 to 2 inches in a short time. Warning criteria sleet accumulations were met during the late afternoon. The sleet transitioned to an area of very heavy snow during the late afternoon and evening which eventually focused into a heavier band with lighter snows on either side. Winds occasionally gusted to 30 to 40 mph during the time of heaviest snow. The heavy snow and blowing snow reduced visibilities below a quarter mile at times during the late afternoon into the evening creating hazardous driving conditions. Snow intensities diminished during the evening along with the winds. However...lighter snows persisted into the early morning hours of the 1st. Storm total snow accumulations through the early morning hours of the 1st ranged from 4 to 8 inches within the heavier band...with 3 to 5 inch accumulations on either side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. This was the continuation of an event which began during the afternoon of March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region from the late morning through the afternoon hours of the 26th along with an upper level disturbance and cooling temperature aloft. Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front during the late morning and persisted through late afternoon. The severe thunderstorms produced large hail. A weak tornado  was also reported near Ludlow in southeast Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from nickel to quarter size. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and heavy rains contributed to rapid snow melt and ice movement during mid April. Rising river levels and ice jams led to flooding across portions of northern and eastern Maine. High temperatures on the 14th generally ranged from around 60 to the mid 60s north...to the mid 60s Downeast. These temperatures produced rapid snow melt which led to rising river and stream levels. The rising rivers caused ice to move which led to ice jams. Heavy rains then occurred across the region from the 15th into the 16th in advance of a cold front...with totals of 1.00 to 2.00 inches. Runoff from this rain...in addition to the melting snow...further contributed to rising river levels and ice jams.|River and stream flooding began to develop during the afternoon and evening of the 15th related to the rapid runoff due to snow melt and heavy rains along with ice jams. Runoff from fields and hills overwhelmed culverts and poor drainage areas which contributed to flooding and road closures along with the only fatality of the event. |Significant flooding across Piscataquis and Washington counties lasted into the afternoon of the 17th.  Across Aroostook and Penobscot counties...significant flooding lasted into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and melting snow contributed to flooding and several road closures. Flooding began to develop during the evening of the 15th...lasting into the afternoon of the 17th. Road closures included Route 6 near Lambert Lake and portions of Route 169 between Danforth and Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures and heavy rains contributed to rapid snow melt and ice movement during mid April. Rising river levels and ice jams led to flooding across portions of northern and eastern Maine. High temperatures on the 14th generally ranged from around 60 to the mid 60s north...to the mid 60s Downeast. These temperatures produced rapid snow melt which led to rising river and stream levels. The rising rivers caused ice to move which led to ice jams. Heavy rains then occurred across the region from the 15th into the 16th in advance of a cold front...with totals of 1.00 to 2.00 inches. Runoff from this rain...in addition to the melting snow...further contributed to rising river levels and ice jams.|River and stream flooding began to develop during the afternoon and evening of the 15th related to the rapid runoff due to snow melt and heavy rains along with ice jams. Runoff from fields and hills overwhelmed culverts and poor drainage areas which contributed to flooding and road closures along with the only fatality of the event. |Significant flooding across Piscataquis and Washington counties lasted into the afternoon of the 17th.  Across Aroostook and Penobscot counties...significant flooding lasted into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mild temperatures of around 60 to the mid 60s caused rapid snow melt and runoff during the 14th which led to rising river and stream levels. Heavy rain then occurred during the 15th in advance of a slow moving cold front along with continued mild temperatures which contributed to further runoff. Rainfall totals during the 15th generally ranged from 1.00 to 2.00 inches. |Flooding began to develop during the afternoon of the 15th due to rising river and stream levels along with ice jams. Additional flooding was due to runoff from fields and hills which overwhelmed culverts and produced standing water in poor drainage areas. Road closures due to both types of flooding began to develop during the afternoon of the 15th. The number of road closures then steadily increased across the entire county through the remainder of the 15th into the 16th...with numerous road closures persisting into the 18th. |Flooding occurred along portions of both the Aroostook River and Saint John River. Numerous roads were closed around Fort Fairfield...on the Aroostook River...starting on the afternoon of the 15th due to elevated river levels and several ice jams. The final ice jams released during the afternoon of the 16th allowing water levels to recede.  Several forecast points along the Aroostook River reached flood stage. An ice jam caused the river at Washburn to briefly spike above flood stage for around 2 hours during the overnight hours of the 15th into the 16th...cresting around 2.5 feet above flood stage. At Masardis...the river rose above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 16th...crested around a foot above flood stage that afternoon...then fell below flood stage later that evening. Elevated river levels and ice jams contributed to flooding along portions of the Saint John River. An ice jam affected the Saint Francis area during the morning of the 16th resulting in minor flooding. A more significant ice jam...estimated to be 3 to 4 miles long...then affected the Saint Francis area during the 17th into the early morning of the 18th before releasing. This ice jam closed portions of Route 161 between Saint Francis and Allagash due to water and large chunks of ice covering the road. This ice jam also caused the voluntary evacuation of 5 homes along the river for precautionary reasons. When this ice jam released the ice contributed to elevated river levels further downstream...from Hamlin to Van Buren...where portions of Route 1A were briefly closed. Many small streams also overflowed across the county contributing to flooding. Other towns affected by flooding and road closures included Bridgewater...Caribou...Castle Hill...Easton...Hodgdon...Houlton...Mapleton...Presque Isle...Westfield and Wytopitlock.  |Non river flooding also occurred across the region due to the runoff of heavy rain and snow melt from fields and hills. This runoff overwhelmed culverts which led to flooding and road closures...with flooding also occurring in poor drainage areas. The one fatality during this event was due to runoff from fields which had flooded a dirt road in the Caswell area. After moving his vehicle...the individual attempted to walk back across the flooded road and stepped into the fast moving water of a culvert which swept him away resulting in his drowning. This fatality occurred during the evening of the 15th. The monetary damage represents a total from 4 towns. Most of this damage was related to flooding from fields.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding due to runoff from heavy rain and snow melt...along with ice jams...began to develop during the evening of the 15th. Flooding then persisted into the afternoon of the 17th. The Dover-Foxcroft forecast point on the Piscataquis River rose above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 16th...crested almost 2 feet above flood stage later that morning...then fell back below flood stage later that evening. Road closures were reported in the towns of Abbot...Brownville...Greenville...Milo...Sangerville and Willamantic. A bridge over the Sebec River...in the town of Sebec...was closed. A bridge over the Piscataquis River in Sangerville was also closed. Flooding was also reported along portions of the Pleasant River. The monetary damages are from 2 towns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front spread rain across the region during the evening hours of May 16th. The rain continued through the night and ended by noon on the 17th. In general 2 to 3 inches of rain fell with a maximum of 3.41 inches reported in the mountains of Maine. This caused minor flooding on the Kennebec and Swift Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dumped 2 to 3 inches of rain across the mountains of Maine causing minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs), which crested at 43,737 cfs.

This flooding led to evacuations along Lincoln Street...Liberty Street and Water Street.|Flooding occurred at several forecast points along the Penobscot River. At West Enfield the river rose above flood stage during the afternoon of the 16th...crested just under a foot above flood stage later that afternoon...then fell back below flood stage during the evening of the 17th. At Eddington the river briefly rose barely above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 18th. An ice jam on the East Branch of the Penobscot River during the afternoon of the 16th caused flooding which closed Route 11 between Grindstone and Soldierton. Route 2 near Greenbush was closed due to flooding during the morning of the 17th.  Flooding along the Stillwater River in Orono reached several homes along the river and flooded a portion of a University of Maine parking lot through the morning and afternoon of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front spread rain across the region during the evening hours of May 16th. The rain continued through the night and ended by noon on the 17th. In general 2 to 3 inches of rain fell with a maximum of 3.41 inches reported in the mountains of Maine. This caused minor flooding on the Kennebec and Swift Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dumped 2 to 3 inches of rain across the mountains of Maine resulting in minor flooding on the Swift River at Roxbury (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 9.31 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the region from the late morning through the afternoon hours of the 26th along with an upper level disturbance and cooling temperature aloft. Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of the front during the late morning and persisted through late afternoon. The severe thunderstorms produced large hail. A weak tornado  was also reported near Ludlow in southeast Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado briefly touched down producing minor tree damage along a portion of Ludlow Road...with trees uprooted and split (DI 27, DI 28, DOD3). Maximum winds were estimated at around 75 mph. The tornado was produced from a mini-supercell thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy precipitation developed across central and coastal portions of Washington county within the deformation zone of an upper low during the afternoon of March 31st. Precipitation early in the afternoon was a mix of light rain and freezing rain. Temperatures aloft cooled while the precipitation intensified allowing an initial transition to heavy sleet and then heavy snow through the mid to late afternoon hours. Sleet rapidly accumulated 1 to 2 inches in a short time. Warning criteria sleet accumulations were met during the late afternoon. The sleet transitioned to an area of very heavy snow during the late afternoon and evening which eventually focused into a heavier band with lighter snows on either side. Winds occasionally gusted to 30 to 40 mph during the time of heaviest snow. The heavy snow and blowing snow reduced visibilities below a quarter mile at times during the late afternoon into the evening creating hazardous driving conditions. Snow intensities diminished during the evening along with the winds. However...lighter snows persisted into the early morning hours of the 1st. Storm total snow accumulations through the early morning hours of the 1st ranged from 4 to 8 inches within the heavier band...with 3 to 5 inch accumulations on either side of the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 8 inches...along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. This was the continuation of an event which began during the afternoon of March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures occurred during the 2nd in advance of a slow moving cold front. Severe thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon hours of the 2nd and continued into the evening. The thunderstorms were most concentrated in an area extending from Piscataquis county...across northern and central portions of Penobscot county...to eastern Aroostook county. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds along with isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled in Abbot by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The post tropical remnants of what had once been Hurricane Arthur brought high winds and heavy rain to eastern Maine. The strongest winds occurred through the morning hours of the 5th across Washington and Hancock counties. Peak wind gusts across Washington and Hancock counties generally ranged from around 40 mph up to around 60 mph...with the highest gusts generally across Washington county nearest the track of the low. A peak wind gust of 71 mph was reported at Machias Seal Island well offshore from Washington county. Extensive tree damage occurred across Washington and Hancock counties with trees uprooted or snapped along with numerous broken branches. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines and utility poles which led to extensive power outages. At the peak of the event...20000 to 25000 customers lost power across mostly Washington and Hancock counties with lesser power outages across Penobscot county. Some customers across Washington county were without power for several days.|Another area of damaging winds developed across portions of eastern Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 5th where heavy rain helped mix strong winds aloft to the surface. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph occurred across portions of eastern Aroostook county where extensive tree damage also occurred with many trees uprooted or snapped along with many broken branches and scattered power outages. Fully leafed trees and wet ground enhanced the impact of the tree damage from the winds across all of eastern Maine.  |Heavy rain also occurred across portions of Eastern Maine. Abundant moisture was drawn north along a stalled frontal boundary in advance of the low. Heavy rain developed across portions of Washington and Hancock counties during the evening of the 4th and persisted into the afternoon of the 5th. Event total rainfall across Washington county generally ranged from 3.00 to 5.00 inches...with localized totals of 6.00 to 6.50 inches. Rainfall totals across Hancock county generally ranged from 3.00 to 4.00 inches. Minor small stream flooding along with ponding of water in poor drainage areas occurred across Washington and Hancock counties. Heavy rains also developed across eastern Aroostook county from the overnight hours of the 4th into the morning of the 5th and persisted into the afternoon. Rainfall totals across eastern Aroostook county generally ranged from 1.50 to 3.50 inches...with localized totals of up to 4.00 to 4.50 inches. Ponding of water in poor drainage areas was reported across portions of eastern Aroostook county...though no significant flooding issues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts produced extensive tree damage with many trees uprooted or snapped along with numerous broken branches. The falling trees and branches helped bring down power lines and utility poles which led to numerous power outages. Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 40 mph to around 60 mph. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded near Jonesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine and southeast Maine through the night of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Snow began during the afternoon of the 13th...then continued through the afternoon of the 14th. Warning criteria snow accumulations were reached from the late evening hours of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Storm total snow accumulations across east-central areas ranged from 7 to 11 inches. Snow accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with locally up to 17 inches across southeast Aroostook county. Lesser snow accumulations occurred across central and Downeast areas where enough warmer air was drawn north to allow the precipitation to mix with sleet and freezing rain. Precipitation eventually transitioned to all rain in some locations Downeast. Snow accumulations across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches...below warning criteria...before the transition to a wintry mix. However...the heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines did contribute to power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county where around 4300 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches...below warning criteria...before the heavy wet snow transitioned to a wintry mix or rain.

Lesser snow accumulations occurred across central and Downeast areas where enough warmer air was drawn north to allow the precipitation to mix with sleet and freezing rain. Snow accumulations across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches...below warning criteria...before the transition to a wintry mix. However...the heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines did contribute to power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county where around 4300 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked east of the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from the overnight hours of the 15th through the morning of the 16th. Snow began during the evening of the 15th...continued through the overnight hours and morning of the 16th...then diminished during the afternoon. The storm brought heavy snow to Washington and Hancock counties. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with localized totals of up to 20 inches with bands of heavier snow. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met during the early morning hours of the 16th. Occasional wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the northern Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 12th tracked northeastward to the Carolina coast by the morning of the 13th spreading snow across the region.  The low continued to move slowly northeastward and was near Portland by the morning of the 14th and then moved quickly into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 15 as another storm approached from the south.  The low brought heavy snow to the entire state with some mixed precipitation on the 14th, especially along the coast.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 14 inches.

Arctic high pressure which had begun to build across the region during late December persisted into early January. Temperatures from the 1st into the morning of the 4th averaged 25 to 40 degrees below normal. The coldest overnight low temperatures generally occurred during the morning of the 2nd with readings ranging from 25 below to around 40 below zero across northern areas with 10 below to 20 below zero Downeast. A record daily low temperature of 28 below zero was recorded at Caribou during the morning of the 2nd...with a record daily low of 19 below zero established at Bangor. Otherwise...low temperatures during this time generally ranged from 20 below to 35 below zero across northern areas...with 5 below to 15 below zero Downeast. Temperatures remained below zero for 48 to 72 hours across portions of northern Maine during this time. The coldest day also occurred on the 2nd when high temperatures across northern areas generally held in the 10 below to 20 below zero range...with single digits below zero Downeast. Winds occasionally combined with the extreme cold to produce wind chills of 45 below to 55 below zero across northern areas and 30 below to 40 below zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought extremely cold temperatures to the region at the start of January. Dangerous wind chills also occurred at times. This was the continuation of an extreme cold event which began at the end of December.

Winds occasionally combined with the extreme cold to produce wind chills of 45 below to 55 below zero across northern areas and 30 below to 40 below zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure brought extremely cold temperatures to the region at the start of January. Dangerous wind chills also occurred at times. This was the continuation of an extreme cold event which began at the end of December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure which had begun to build across the region during late December persisted into early January. Temperatures from the 1st into the morning of the 4th averaged 25 to 40 degrees below normal. The coldest overnight low temperatures generally occurred during the morning of the 2nd with readings ranging from 25 below to around 40 below zero across northern areas with 10 below to 20 below zero Downeast. A record daily low temperature of 28 below zero was recorded at Caribou during the morning of the 2nd...with a record daily low of 19 below zero established at Bangor. Otherwise...low temperatures during this time generally ranged from 20 below to 35 below zero across northern areas...with 5 below to 15 below zero Downeast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex area of low pressure intensified as it slowly moved off the Mid Atlantic coastline on January 2nd. It continued to reorganize (975 mb) and move south of Cape Cod on the 3rd. Gale force northerly winds on January 3rd combined with building|nearshore waves up to 15 feet to produce splash-over and coastal flooding near the time of high tide. Winds switched to the north during the midday high tide on the 3rd. However...the highest astronomical tides of the month, combined with a modest storm surge to impact the coast from Portland south through the New Hampshire Seacoast.||In Portland, automobiles drove through water a foot deep on side streets adjacent to the wharfs. Public Works closed Sawyer Road in Scarborough due to flooding. In Biddeford, Granite Point Road was flooded up to 10 inches deep for a stretch of road 1700 feet long, while water covered a portion of Hills Beach and Miles Stretch Roads. In Saco, minor flooding occurred on Surf Street and objects became covered in ice due to the splashing waves in the near zero degree temperatures. Further to the south in Kennebunk, Beach Avenue was closed due to splash-over and road flooding. In Wells, roads were flooded at Mile Road, Bourne Avenue and Furbish and water was up to four feet deep along Webhannet Drive. In York, considerable splash-over occurred at the southern end of Long Beach tossing debris onto the roads.

It continued to reorganize (975 mb) and move south of Cape Cod on the 3rd. Winds switched to the north during the midday high tide on the 3rd. However...the highest astronomical tides of the month, combined with a modest storm surge to impact the coast from Portland south through the New Hampshire Seacoast.||In Portland, automobiles drove through water a foot deep on side streets adjacent to the wharfs. Public Works closed Sawyer Road in Scarborough due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine and southeast Maine through the night of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Snow began during the afternoon of the 13th...then continued through the afternoon of the 14th. Warning criteria snow accumulations were reached from the late evening hours of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. Storm total snow accumulations across east-central areas ranged from 7 to 11 inches. Snow accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with locally up to 17 inches across southeast Aroostook county. Lesser snow accumulations occurred across central and Downeast areas where enough warmer air was drawn north to allow the precipitation to mix with sleet and freezing rain. Precipitation eventually transitioned to all rain in some locations Downeast. Snow accumulations across central and Downeast areas generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches...below warning criteria...before the transition to a wintry mix. However...the heavy wet snow weighing down branches and power lines did contribute to power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Hancock county where around 4300 customers lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked east of the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from the overnight hours of the 15th through the morning of the 16th. Snow began during the evening of the 15th...continued through the overnight hours and morning of the 16th...then diminished during the afternoon. The storm brought heavy snow to Washington and Hancock counties. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 15 inches...with localized totals of up to 20 inches with bands of heavier snow. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met during the early morning hours of the 16th. Occasional wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of July 15th.  Precipitable water values over 2 inches and strong deep layer shear combined with high capes to produce numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding were the main threats on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees blocking Industry Road near Allens Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into a hot and humid late summer air mass resulted in severe convection. Wind damage was the main threat with these storms as they moved into southwestern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon convection. Falling freezing levels contributed to the formation of large hail with many of these storms. All severe reports were exclusively due to hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near West Hollis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass was in place across the region early on the 16th. An isolated severe thunderstorm developed during the early morning hours causing wind damage in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous large trees on Route 117 in Buckfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and tropical air mass was in place across the region ahead of a slow moving cold front on the afternoon of July 2nd. Strong thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening producing damaging winds and some reports of hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Rumford.
